nodes.title: Loads on the System
nodes.note:
CIRA wants an Application Ecology Layer 🌿

Reducer/Core + CIRA gives you an excellent pattern for one coherent editable world:

state → action → reducer → render
plus undo/redo, inspection, persistence, and canvas manipulation.

But now you’re trying to build systems where there are:

* multiple windows
* summonable tools
* hypertextual navigation
* component reuse
* LLM-generated submodules
* graphical tutorials
* runtime testing
* configuration/storage/app lifecycle concerns
* maybe one global state, maybe many local states

That’s not a failure of CIRA. That’s a sign that CIRA is a lower layer, and you now need a layer above it.

nodes.title: Does tkinter support declaring that a given tkinter.Toplevel is modal?
TkAppFrame / Application Ecology Layer
nodes.note:
It owns things like:

* window registry
* summonable windows
* persistent app storage
* configuration
* runtime services
* testing gateway
* LLM-friendly module contracts
* tutorial overlays
* window lifecycle
* inter-window messaging
* tick/update loop


Application services:
* config
* storage
* logging
* command dispatch
* runtime tick
* window registry
* event bus
* test gateway
* tutorial overlay registry

Window services
* summon window
* close window
* focus window
* find existing instance
* register widgets
* register tutorial targets
* register test handles

CIRA services?
* create reducer core
* attach undo/redo
* route Ctrl+Z / Ctrl+Y
* serialize document state
* dispatch action
* observe state changes

nodes.title: System Configuration
nodes.note:
* configuration from within the application
  (perhaps automatically built out dialog)

* configuration from the command line

nodes.title: Persistent App Storage
nodes.note:
{
  "key-0001": {...},
  "key-0002": {...},
  ...,
  "anonymous": [{...}, ...]
}


get_data(key/i)
set_data(key/i, ...)
new_data(data)  -> i (anonymous data only)

nodes.title: Window Summoning Policy
nodes.note:
Every window definition should declare its summon policy.

Something like:

WINDOW_DEF = {
    "WINDOW_TYPE": "asset_browser",
    "TITLE": "Asset Browser",
    "SUMMON_POLICY": "singleton",
}

or:

WINDOW_DEF = {
    "WINDOW_TYPE": "object_inspector",
    "TITLE": "Inspector",
    "SUMMON_POLICY": "per-key",
    "KEY_KIND": "object_guid",
}

Then the app frame handles it.

Possible summon policies:

Policy	Meaning
"singleton"	Only one instance exists. Summon brings it forward.
"per-key"	One instance per key, such as object GUID, document path, asset ID.
"free"	Every summon creates a new window.
"embedded"	Not a Toplevel; can be mounted inside another frame.
"modal"	Temporary blocking interaction window.


This gives you the exact thing you wanted: one imported window definition can behave correctly in different apps, because it publishes how it expects to be summoned.

---

This part matters because it determines lifecycle and storage behavior.

Examples:

Window	Policy
Main window	singleton
Asset browser	singleton
Object inspector	per-key
Search results	free or per-query
Preferences	singleton or modal
Rename dialog	modal
Embedded toolbar	embedded
Embedded property pane	embedded

nodes.title: Tutorial Overlays
nodes.note:
"But you can still get 80% of the effect inside a normal Tk app."

For each Toplevel, create a transparent-ish overlay Toplevel or Canvas layer that sits above the window.

You can use:

winfo_rootx()
winfo_rooty()
winfo_width()
winfo_height()

to locate widgets on screen, then draw highlight boxes/arrows relative to them.

This supports things like:

Dim this window.
Highlight the Tags field.
Draw arrow to the Hook button.
Show explanatory bubble.
Wait for user click.

Here is what I am.
Here is how I am summoned.
Here are the events I publish.
Here are the events I listen for.
Here are the app services I need.
Here are my test/tutorial targets.

│

    ├── documents
    ├── logs

    └── background-ish timers

nodes.title: First Build Goals
nodes.note:
What you are targeting today

Your present target is the minimum viable TkVillage kernel.

Not CIRA itself.
Not tutorial overlays.
Not a visual builder.
Not a full plugin system.
Not a game engine.

You are building the host organism.

TkVillage Kernel

It should include:

Subsystem	Purpose
App object / global app dictionary	shared runtime context
project directory	.foo/ style local app data root
configuration system	named, typed config parameters
CLI interface	inspect/set config, run tests, launch app
window registry	register window modules
summon system	open/focus/create windows by name/key
window policies	singleton, per-key, free, embedded, modal
lifecycle hooks	create, show, close, destroy
tick loop	periodic runtime heartbeat
message bus	inter-window/module communication
testing gateway	run app in test mode inside tkinter mainloop
LLM module contract	simple documented pattern for window modules

That’s enough. That’s already a coherent first architecture.

nodes.title: Envisioned Initial Code
nodes.note:
import tkvillage as village
import windows.main_window as main_window
import windows.asset_browser as asset_browser
import windows.object_inspector as object_inspector

g = {
    "quit": False,
}

def main():
    app = village.make_app(
        app_name="data_garden",
        project_dir=".data_garden",
        g=g,
    )

    village.define_config(app, "autosave_seconds", int, 10)
    village.define_config(app, "theme", str, "default")

    village.register_window(app, main_window.WINDOW_DEF)
    village.register_window(app, asset_browser.WINDOW_DEF)
    village.register_window(app, object_inspector.WINDOW_DEF)

    village.run_cli_or_app(app)


---

my feedback:
* The declarative dimension of this -- that's correct.
* But it's not object oriented.  At all.
* And you don't call make_app, because, dufus,
  if you're using village, there's an APP.
  You just use the global space, because all tkvillage
  programs are apps using the tkvillage.
* The tkvillage system should
  have it's own g["quit"] just by default, defaulting to False.
  You don't have to specify that.
  Why make the user do something that they could get wrong, for no reason?

I would expect more like:


---
import tkvillage as village
import windows.main_window as main_window
import windows.asset_browser as asset_browser
import windows.object_inspector as object_inspector

def setup_app():
    village.define_app({"name": "data_garden",
                        "title": "Data Garden",
                        "dir": ".data_garden"})
    village.define_config({"name": "autosave_seconds",
                           "type": "int",
                           "title": "Autosave (in seconds)",
                           "default": "10"})
    village.register_module(mainwindow)
    village.register_module(asset_browser)
    village.register_module(object_inspector)
    
    village.run()

---

register_module would then locate the WINDOW_DEF within it.

village.run() will read the command line arguments, and determine
whether to:

(a) present help information (and if so, what help information)
(b) process the command and print response (for example, "datagarden set autosave_seconds 10" will just respond with an "Affirmative," and confirmation that autosave_seconds has been set to 10)
(c) start the GUI and run the program
(d) start the GUI and run test routines

nodes.title: Shape of a Window Module
nodes.note:
OK, so Wing-Cat Proposed:

---
WINDOW_DEF = {
    "NAME": "object_inspector",
    "TITLE": "Object Inspector",
    "POLICY": "per-key",
    "KEY_NAME": "object_guid",
    "REQUIRES": ["storage", "messages"],
    "PUBLISHES": ["object-updated"],
    "SUBSCRIBES": ["selection-changed"],
}

def create(app, parent, key=None):
    ...
    return {
        "root": toplevel_or_frame,
        "close": close,
        "tick": tick,
        "focus": focus,
    }
---

My thoughts:

* REQUIRES/PUBLISHES/SUBSCRIBES -- this is all going to depend on the communication system, which I am uncommitted to at this time.

Dictated:
---
Remember that we're not using object-oriented programming. Everything is declarative. Uh, when a event is hit on a window, like say a menu item is chosen, the mouse moves through it, um, wherever an event occurs, it's going to pass through the village's event handler, the TK village events handler. And the TK village's event handler is going to identify which window the event appeared on, and it's going to activate the window's data structures. What I mean by activating it is just that there will be some global pointers that say, this is the window that is the window of concern here. It should be possible to get to all the data for all the windows that aren't active, uh, but when the event handler within the user's module code for that window is hit, uh, the TK village system will have already activated that window. It's not going to pass it. Uh, so when the handler responds, it should always assume, uh, when the end handler responds, it should always assume that TK village has already put the poke into, you know, its registers or whatever, the current address for the current window. This matters a lot because you're gonna have window code that is, has the policy that it's per key, right? And so when that event handler is running, the village system will have already poked in the right object GUID and the right pointers to the window data structures. So, you know, but it's not object-oriented. That's really critical to understand. It's contextually based. Uh, and remember, um, these methods, these functions, they take at most two parameters and optionally some flags. Uh, so typically they'll just receive, there'll be nullary, which means we're just relying on the context to tell us everything about the situation, or they'll have like one parameter. And remember, my conviction is that a parameter is something that actually meaningfully changes. I don't use parameters to convey configuration information. That's what the context is for. So that applies to this system as well.  Maybe even: especially.
---

nodes.title: Process Notes
nodes.note:
Define the Following:

* App -- title, name, description, project folder, ...
* Project Folder structure
* Configuration Data -- configuration options, titles, identifiers, types, defaults, storage location, ...
* Window Declaration & Invocation -- how you declare a window, how you create a window
* Inter-Module Communications System -- what model defines communication, how messages are delivered, ...
* Run-time Core Loop

nodes.title: Originating Discussion: tkinter Conventions
nodes.note:
This conversation began from a different place, actually --
a discussion of common tkinter conventions.

And it evolved into: "Well, we should make a framework out of this."

Interestingly enough, what came out looked almost EXACTLY like the First Build Goals..!

nodes.title: Eliminating unnecessary variables: "rt", but also... window record.
Directive: Revise TkVillage Window Callback Context Model
Directive: Revise TkVillage Window Callback Context Mmodel
nodes.note:
# Directive: Revise TkVillage Window Callback Context Model

Update TkVillage so that window callbacks no longer receive `rt` or `record` as explicit arguments.

This change is intentional and architectural. TkVillage should follow Lion’s programming style: contextual system state belongs in module-level globals, not in ceremonial callback parameters. Pass only what varies. The runtime module is already available as a module, and the active window record should be available through the runtime’s current-context state.

## 1. Remove the `rt` callback argument entirely

The runtime module should not be passed into callbacks.

Change callback signatures from forms like:

```python
create(rt, record)
make_initial_state(rt, key=None, payload=None)
reduce_event(rt, state, event)
project(rt, record)
on_show(rt, record)
on_close(rt, record)
on_destroy(rt, record)
on_tick(rt, record)
```

to forms like:

```python
create()
make_initial_state(key=None, payload=None)
reduce_event(state, event)
project()
on_show()
on_close()
on_destroy()
on_tick()
```

If callback code needs access to the runtime, it should import the runtime module directly:

```python
import tkvillage.app as rt
```

or use the public module import pattern already established by TkVillage.

Do not pass `rt` as an argument. Access the module as a module.

## 2. Remove the `record` callback argument entirely

The live window record should also not be passed into callbacks.

Instead, TkVillage should maintain the current active window record in:

```python
rt.g["record"]
```

This means:

```python
def create():
    record = rt.g["record"]
```

or simply:

```python
record = rt.g["record"]
```

inside the relevant function body.

The meaning of `rt.g["record"]` is:

> The live window record that the TkVillage runtime is currently considering, invoking, projecting, reducing, creating, showing, closing, destroying, or ticking.

This is not an arbitrary global. It is the current runtime focus.

## 3. Enforce the single-active-window invocation invariant

This change depends on a critical runtime invariant:

> Only one window’s code may be actively running at a time.

When TkVillage invokes code for window A, the runtime is acting as window A. During that invocation, `rt.g["record"]` must refer to window A’s record. No other window’s code may be synchronously entered while window A’s code is running.

This applies to:

```python
create()
make_initial_state()
reduce_event()
project()
on_show()
on_close()
on_destroy()
on_tick()
```

and any other window-kind callback.

The runtime should set `rt.g["record"]` immediately before invoking a window callback, and clear or restore it immediately afterward.

Use a disciplined helper for this, for example:

```python
def call_with_record(record, fn, *args):
    previous = rt.g.get("record")
    rt.g["record"] = record
    try:
        return fn(*args)
    finally:
        rt.g["record"] = previous
```

If the system wants to be stricter, it may assert that `previous` is `None` before setting the current record. The main architectural rule is that nested cross-window invocation should not occur.

## 4. Cross-window action must be queued, not immediate

While window A is active, it must not synchronously command, mutate, project, create, destroy, close, raise, or otherwise cause window B’s code to run.

Forbidden from inside current-window code:

```python
summon_window("other-kind")        # if this immediately creates/runs another window
close_window(other_window_id)
destroy_window(other_window_id)
project_window(other_window_id)
other_record["state"]["x"] = 10
other_record["widgets"]["x"].configure(...)
```

Instead, any request for another window to do something must become an event, effect, message, or queued runtime request.

Allowed pattern:

```python
return state, [
    {
        "type": "SUMMON_WINDOW",
        "window_kind": "other-kind",
        "payload": {...}
    }
]
```

or:

```python
return state, [
    {
        "type": "POST_EVENT",
        "target_id": other_window_id,
        "event": {"type": "REFRESH_REQUESTED"}
    }
]
```

The runtime may perform those actions later, after the current active window invocation has completed and `rt.g["record"]` is no longer pointing at the original window.

The runtime should behave like a small operating system:

```text
set current record to window A
run A's callback/reducer/projection
collect requests/effects
clear current record

later:

set current record to window B
run B's callback/reducer/projection
clear current record
```

## 5. Read-only cross-window queries are allowed

There is one important exception:

A window may ask for information about another window, provided the operation is strictly read-only and does not cause the target window to act.

Allowed:

```python
get_window(other_window_id)
get_window_state(other_window_id)
list_windows()
list_windows_by_kind("asset-browser")
peek_window_value(other_window_id, path)
```

These functions should take the target window id, window kind, instance key, or other identifier as an explicit argument.

Reason:

> Random-access read-only inspection is okay. Cross-window command/action/mutation is not.

The distinction is:

```text
ambient current context:
    What window am I?

explicit read-only query:
    Tell me about that other window.

queued request:
    Ask that other window or the runtime to do something later.
```

So explicit arguments are still appropriate for read-only random-access query functions. They are not operating on the current window; they are inspecting some selected external object.

## 6. Update documentation and examples

Update the system manual, API reference, data dictionary, and examples so the canonical callback signatures no longer include `rt` or `record`.

Preferred examples should look like:

```python
import tkinter as tk
import tkvillage as village
import tkvillage.app as rt

MAIN_WINDOW = "main"
INCREMENT_CLICKED = "INCREMENT_CLICKED"

def make_initial_state(key=None, payload=None):
    return {"count": 0, "status": "Ready"}

def create():
    record = rt.g["record"]

    label = tk.Label(record["toplevel"])
    label.grid(row=0, column=0)

    button = tk.Button(
        record["toplevel"],
        text="Increment",
        command=lambda: village.post_event_to_window(
            record["window_id"],
            {"type": INCREMENT_CLICKED}
        )
    )
    button.grid(row=1, column=0)

    record["widgets"]["label"] = label

def reduce_event(state, event):
    if event["type"] == INCREMENT_CLICKED:
        new_state = dict(state)
        new_state["count"] += 1
        new_state["status"] = f"Clicked {new_state['count']} time(s)"
        return new_state, [{"type": "LOG", "message": "incremented"}]

    return state, []

def project():
    record = rt.g["record"]
    record["widgets"]["label"].configure(text=record["state"]["status"])
```

## 7. Preserve or provide a temporary compatibility path only if necessary

If existing tests/examples currently depend on the old callback signatures, it is acceptable to add a short-lived compatibility layer during migration.

However, the target model is not “callbacks may optionally accept `rt` and `record`.”

The target model is:

```python
create()
make_initial_state(key=None, payload=None)
reduce_event(state, event)
project()
```

with:

```python
rt.g["record"]
```

as the current active window record.

Once the migration is complete, remove old-signature support unless there is a strong reason to keep it.

## 8. Summary of the new rule

TkVillage callback context should work like this:

```text
The runtime module is global/module-level.
The current active window record is rt.g["record"].
Only one window invocation may be active at a time.
Read-only inspection of other windows is allowed by explicit id/key arguments.
Cross-window action or mutation must be expressed as queued work: event, message, effect, or request.
```

The point of the change is not merely shorter function signatures. The point is to make TkVillage’s execution model clear:

> The runtime acts as one window at a time. That window is the current record. Other windows may be inspected, but not commanded directly.

nodes.title: Five Diagrams
nodes.note:
The thing you’re proposing is not “more clever callbacks.” It is:

> **TkVillage should make windows operate like controlled machines, with input ports, queues/facts, tick phases, reducers, and projections — instead of letting raw Tkinter callbacks improvise behavior all over the place.**

Claude’s attached text gets at the same split: chrome inputs become semantic events, manipulation surfaces update tick-sampled facts, both go through village registration helpers, and no port performs app behavior directly. 

Here are the **five diagrams** I think you should make.

---

## 1. The Window Machine Diagram

**Purpose:** Understand what one TkVillage window *is*.

Draw one large box titled:

```text
Window Record / Window Machine
```

Inside it, put these compartments:

```text
identity
  window_id
  window_kind
  instance_key

Tk shell
  toplevel
  widgets

state
  reducer-owned semantic state

raw / facts
  pointer
  buttons
  modifiers
  motion_points
  previous/current

queues
  event_queue
  message_queue maybe

lifecycle flags
  needs_project
  is_open
  dirty
```

Then draw arrows:

```text
Tk input ports → raw/facts OR event_queue
event_queue → reducer → state
state → project → widgets
raw/facts → tokenizer/continuity engine → semantic events or transient behavior
```

The point of this diagram is to see: **a window is a machine with compartments.** Tkinter widgets are not the app. The reducer state is not the whole window. RAW facts are not semantic events. The queue is not immediate behavior.

This should calm a lot of the anxiety, because it shows the machine as one thing.

---

## 2. The Tick Phase Diagram

**Purpose:** Understand “controlled phases.”

Draw a vertical flowchart:

```text
TICK START
  ↓
1. Capture / promote RAW facts
   previous ← current
   current ← sampled toolkit facts
   include accumulated edge facts
   include motion_points since last tick
  ↓
2. Tokenizers / continuity engines inspect RAW
   compute pressed, released, moving, dragging, threshold crossed
  ↓
3. Drain event queues
   reducer(state, event) → new_state, effects
  ↓
4. Clear current record before cross-window effects
  ↓
5. Route effects / queued requests
   summon, close, send event, log, etc.
  ↓
6. Project dirty windows
  ↓
7. Clear per-tick buffers
   motion_points = []
  ↓
TICK END
```

This diagram answers your minor thought about mouse motion points:

> The motion-points buffer should be available during the tick to the relevant window’s processing, then cleared **after the tick phase that consumes it**, not after the first arbitrary reader.

So not “first window looks, then delete.” More like:

```text
window-local motion_points are part of that window’s RAW input for this tick.
They are cleared at the controlled end-of-tick cleanup phase.
```

If multiple subsystems in the same window need those points, they should all read them before the cleanup phase. That is a phase-design issue, not a callback issue.

---

## 3. The Input Port Taxonomy Diagram

**Purpose:** Stop mixing up events, facts, and raw escape hatches.

Draw three columns:

```text
EVENT PORTS
  button click
  menu item
  WM_DELETE_WINDOW
  Ctrl+S command
  dialog choice

FACT PORTS
  pointer x/y
  button currently down
  Ctrl currently held
  window size
  motion_points during stroke

RAW PORTS
  special adapter code
  unusual Tk callback
  experimental tool behavior
```

Under each column, write what it may do:

```text
EVENT PORT
  translates Tk signal → semantic event
  posts to queue
  does not mutate app state

FACT PORT
  updates RAW/facts
  sampled/promoted by tick
  does not decide meaning

RAW PORT
  advanced escape hatch
  still wrapped with current record
  must be reviewed carefully
```

Then write the law underneath:

```text
Tk callback code may translate.
Tk callback code may not decide.
```

This is probably the single most important diagram.

Because then you can say:

```text
Save button?
  Event port.

Canvas pointer position?
  Fact port.

Freehand ink path?
  Fact port with motion_points buffer.

Strange external toolkit callback?
  Raw port.
```

No reinvention every time.

---

## 4. The Current Record / Window Focus Diagram

**Purpose:** Understand `rt.g["record"]` without getting haunted by it.

Draw a timeline:

```text
runtime is idle
  rt.g["record"] = None

runtime invokes window A
  rt.g["record"] = A
  run create/project/on_tick/reducer callback/input wrapper
  rt.g["record"] = None

runtime invokes window B
  rt.g["record"] = B
  run create/project/on_tick/reducer callback/input wrapper
  rt.g["record"] = None
```

Then draw a big red prohibition arrow:

```text
A active  ──X──> directly run B code
```

And a green allowed path:

```text
A active
  ↓
emit effect / post event request
  ↓
A finishes
  ↓
runtime later invokes B
```

Then add the exception:

```text
Read-only query is allowed:
  get_window_state(B)
  list_windows_by_kind(...)
  peek facts/state

Command/mutation is not allowed:
  close B now
  project B now
  mutate B state
  configure B widgets
```

This diagram is where your rule becomes legible:

> **Ambient context is for “who am I?” Explicit identifiers are for read-only random access. Queues/effects are for asking someone else to do something.**

That’s the doctrine.

---

## 5. The Chrome vs Manipulation Surface Diagram

**Purpose:** Resolve the `command=` / `.bind()` confusion.

Draw two big areas of a GUI:

```text
CHROME
  toolbar
  buttons
  menus
  dialogs
  window close
  keyboard commands like Ctrl+S

MANIPULATION SURFACE
  canvas
  drag handles
  pointer tools
  selection rectangles
  direct object manipulation
  freehand strokes
```

Then write:

```text
Chrome usually emits semantic events directly.
Manipulation surfaces usually update RAW facts.
```

Examples:

```text
Save button click
  → SAVE_REQUESTED event

Delete button click
  → DELETE_SELECTED_REQUESTED event

Canvas mouse position
  → pointer fact

Canvas button down/up
  → button edge facts

Drag movement
  → RAW facts → tokenizer/continuity engine → maybe semantic commit event

Freehand stroke
  → motion_points buffer → stroke engine
```

This diagram is important because the same physical input can mean different architectural things.

`<Button-1>` on a Save button is a semantic event.
`<Button-1>` on a canvas is probably a RAW/fact edge.

So the doctrine is not “all clicks are events” or “all clicks are facts.” The doctrine is:

> **Chrome reports intentions. Manipulation surfaces feed perception.**

That is a very CIRA-shaped distinction.
---

nodes.title: 1. The Window Machine Diagram
nodes.note:
## 1. The Window Machine Diagram

**Purpose:** Understand what one TkVillage window *is*.

Draw one large box titled:

```text
Window Record / Window Machine
```

Inside it, put these compartments:

```text
identity
  window_id
  window_kind
  instance_key

Tk shell
  toplevel
  widgets

state
  reducer-owned semantic state

raw / facts
  pointer
  buttons
  modifiers
  motion_points
  previous/current

queues
  event_queue
  message_queue maybe

lifecycle flags
  needs_project
  is_open
  dirty
```

Then draw arrows:

```text
Tk input ports → raw/facts OR event_queue
event_queue → reducer → state
state → project → widgets
raw/facts → tokenizer/continuity engine → semantic events or transient behavior
```

The point of this diagram is to see: **a window is a machine with compartments.** Tkinter widgets are not the app. The reducer state is not the whole window. RAW facts are not semantic events. The queue is not immediate behavior.

This should calm a lot of the anxiety, because it shows the machine as one thing.

---

nodes.title: 2. The Tick Phase Diagram
nodes.note:
## 2. The Tick Phase Diagram

**Purpose:** Understand “controlled phases.”

Draw a vertical flowchart:

```text
TICK START
  ↓
1. Capture / promote RAW facts
   previous ← current
   current ← sampled toolkit facts
   include accumulated edge facts
   include motion_points since last tick
  ↓
2. Tokenizers / continuity engines inspect RAW
   compute pressed, released, moving, dragging, threshold crossed
  ↓
3. Drain event queues
   reducer(state, event) → new_state, effects
  ↓
4. Clear current record before cross-window effects
  ↓
5. Route effects / queued requests
   summon, close, send event, log, etc.
  ↓
6. Project dirty windows
  ↓
7. Clear per-tick buffers
   motion_points = []
  ↓
TICK END
```

This diagram answers your minor thought about mouse motion points:

> The motion-points buffer should be available during the tick to the relevant window’s processing, then cleared **after the tick phase that consumes it**, not after the first arbitrary reader.

So not “first window looks, then delete.” More like:

```text
window-local motion_points are part of that window’s RAW input for this tick.
They are cleared at the controlled end-of-tick cleanup phase.
```

If multiple subsystems in the same window need those points, they should all read them before the cleanup phase. That is a phase-design issue, not a callback issue.

nodes.title: 3. The Input Port Taxonomy Diagram
nodes.note:
## 3. The Input Port Taxonomy Diagram

**Purpose:** Stop mixing up events, facts, and raw escape hatches.

Draw three columns:

```text
EVENT PORTS
  button click
  menu item
  WM_DELETE_WINDOW
  Ctrl+S command
  dialog choice

FACT PORTS
  pointer x/y
  button currently down
  Ctrl currently held
  window size
  motion_points during stroke

RAW PORTS
  special adapter code
  unusual Tk callback
  experimental tool behavior
```

Under each column, write what it may do:

```text
EVENT PORT
  translates Tk signal → semantic event
  posts to queue
  does not mutate app state

FACT PORT
  updates RAW/facts
  sampled/promoted by tick
  does not decide meaning

RAW PORT
  advanced escape hatch
  still wrapped with current record
  must be reviewed carefully
```

Then write the law underneath:

```text
Tk callback code may translate.
Tk callback code may not decide.
```

This is probably the single most important diagram.

Because then you can say:

```text
Save button?
  Event port.

Canvas pointer position?
  Fact port.

Freehand ink path?
  Fact port with motion_points buffer.

Strange external toolkit callback?
  Raw port.
```

No reinvention every time.

---

nodes.title: 4. The Current Record / Window Focus Diagram
nodes.note:
## 4. The Current Record / Window Focus Diagram

**Purpose:** Understand `rt.g["record"]` without getting haunted by it.

Draw a timeline:

```text
runtime is idle
  rt.g["record"] = None

runtime invokes window A
  rt.g["record"] = A
  run create/project/on_tick/reducer callback/input wrapper
  rt.g["record"] = None

runtime invokes window B
  rt.g["record"] = B
  run create/project/on_tick/reducer callback/input wrapper
  rt.g["record"] = None
```

Then draw a big red prohibition arrow:

```text
A active  ──X──> directly run B code
```

And a green allowed path:

```text
A active
  ↓
emit effect / post event request
  ↓
A finishes
  ↓
runtime later invokes B
```

Then add the exception:

```text
Read-only query is allowed:
  get_window_state(B)
  list_windows_by_kind(...)
  peek facts/state

Command/mutation is not allowed:
  close B now
  project B now
  mutate B state
  configure B widgets
```

This diagram is where your rule becomes legible:

> **Ambient context is for “who am I?” Explicit identifiers are for read-only random access. Queues/effects are for asking someone else to do something.**

That’s the doctrine.

---

nodes.title: 5. The Chrome vs Manipulation Surface Diagram
nodes.note:
## 5. The Chrome vs Manipulation Surface Diagram

**Purpose:** Resolve the `command=` / `.bind()` confusion.

Draw two big areas of a GUI:

```text
CHROME
  toolbar
  buttons
  menus
  dialogs
  window close
  keyboard commands like Ctrl+S

MANIPULATION SURFACE
  canvas
  drag handles
  pointer tools
  selection rectangles
  direct object manipulation
  freehand strokes
```

Then write:

```text
Chrome usually emits semantic events directly.
Manipulation surfaces usually update RAW facts.
```

Examples:

```text
Save button click
  → SAVE_REQUESTED event

Delete button click
  → DELETE_SELECTED_REQUESTED event

Canvas mouse position
  → pointer fact

Canvas button down/up
  → button edge facts

Drag movement
  → RAW facts → tokenizer/continuity engine → maybe semantic commit event

Freehand stroke
  → motion_points buffer → stroke engine
```

This diagram is important because the same physical input can mean different architectural things.

`<Button-1>` on a Save button is a semantic event.
`<Button-1>` on a canvas is probably a RAW/fact edge.

So the doctrine is not “all clicks are events” or “all clicks are facts.” The doctrine is:

> **Chrome reports intentions. Manipulation surfaces feed perception.**

That is a very CIRA-shaped distinction.
---
